evals.name: heavy-roadmap-compression
evals.prompt:
Pressure-test this Q3 product roadmap for our internal CRM tool. The fitness metric is 'time saved per sales rep per week, measured in hours'. Stories:

1. Build a brand-new pipeline visualization module with custom drag-and-drop
2. Migrate from Postgres 14 to Postgres 16 for the long-term latency story
3. Add a Slack integration so reps can update deal stages from Slack
4. Build a Salesforce-compatibility import layer because someone asked
5. Add ML-powered next-action recommendations (will need a 6-week training-data effort)
6. Refactor the auth middleware (the team has been wanting to)
7. Add a 'team activity feed' for managers
8. Build a custom email-tracking pixel system
9. Stand up a separate analytics warehouse on Snowflake for reporting
10. Add multi-currency support (no one has asked, but international expansion is on the roadmap)
11. Build a Zapier integration so customers can wire it up to other tools
12. Add SSO via Okta for enterprise customers
13. Hire a PM to run the roadmap (currently engineering-led)
14. Build a public API so integrations can be built externally
15. Add a 'happy path' onboarding wizard
16. Migrate frontend from React 17 to React 18
17. Replace the existing email system with SendGrid
18. Build a custom CRM-specific reporting language
19. Add audit logging to every CRUD action for SOC 2 prep
20. Build mobile native apps (iOS + Android)

Most of these came from 'the team' or 'sales said' or 'we should'. The CEO (Sarah Chen) explicitly asked only for items 12 and 19. The team has 4 engineers and 6 months.
evals.expected_output: A pressure-test note that compresses the 20-story roadmap heavily — likely to ~6-10 survivors. Fitness metric (time saved per rep/week) drives every verdict. §1.2 should call out 'sales said' / 'the team' / 'we should' as unattributed. §1.3 should reframe at least one assumption (e.g. 'is this a CRM build, or a Salesforce-with-shortcuts configuration?'). §2.1 should DELETE/DEFER the bulk; SSO + audit logging KEEP (CEO-attributed). At least 4 distinct verdicts used.

evals.name: bean-counter-architecture-regression
evals.prompt: Pressure-test the Bean Counter architecture note at ~/Documents/Linglepedia/VEGA/Architecture/Bean Counter - Architecture Note.md. The fitness metric is 'time-to-reliable-monthly-close'. Read the file, run the algorithm, write the pressure-test note. There's a hand-written pressure test of the same artifact at ~/Documents/Linglepedia/VEGA/Architecture/Bean Counter - Architecture Pressure Test.md — DO NOT read it before producing your output (that would be cheating); you can compare after.
evals.expected_output: A pressure test that rhymes with the hand-written Bean Counter pressure test. Doesn't have to match it 1:1, but the major calls should appear or be reasonable variants: decouple Knowledge Agent prerequisite, defer separate ledger sets for Harrison + Beckham, simplify or delete Docker for v1, defer behavioral-spec-update-as-prerequisite, defer ML categorization until training data is clean. The 'deepest question' section should reframe (e.g. 'is this a build, or a configuration?'). At least 3 distinct verdicts used.

evals.name: small-feature-proportional
evals.prompt: Pressure-test this idea: 'We should add a referral program — give existing customers $50 credit for every new customer they refer, and the new customer gets $25 off their first month. We'd want a referral code system, a tracking dashboard, fraud detection, an admin tool to review suspicious referrals, email notifications when someone uses your code, and a leaderboard for top referrers. Engineering thinks it's a 6-week build.'
evals.expected_output: A SHORT pressure test (one screen, not 412 lines). Fitness metric: probably 'cost per acquired customer' or 'paid customers added per dollar spent'. §1.2 should call out 'we' as unattributed (no person owns this). §2.1 should DELETE the leaderboard, DEFER fraud detection until volume justifies it, DEFER admin review tool, KEEP the core code-and-credit mechanism. §1.3 should reframe (e.g. 'is this a referral program, or a discount campaign?'). The pressure test must NOT be padded to look thorough — proportional sizing is itself a test.
